title: Publications
text:
Jack T. Parley, Francesco Cagnetta, and Matthieu Wyart.
Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), PMLR 306, 2026.
This work studies how deep networks learn to parse language-like hierarchical structure from examples alone. We introduce a controlled family of probabilistic context-free grammars with varying tree topology and ambiguity, derive a learning mechanism based on local statistics, and test its predictions in deep networks.

title: Publications
text:
Statistical physics and complex systems

Ductile and brittle yielding of athermal amorphous solids: A mean-field paradigm beyond the random-field Ising model
Jack T. Parley and Peter Sollich.
Physical Review E 110, 045002, 2024.
Mean-field theory of yielding transitions in amorphous solids, developing a paradigm beyond standard random-field-Ising-model descriptions of driven disordered systems.


Mean field description of aging linear response in athermal amorphous solids
Jack T. Parley, Rituparno Mandal, and Peter Sollich.
Physical Review Materials 6, 065601, 2022.
Theory of aging and linear response in athermal amorphous solids, combining mean-field modeling with the physics of slow relaxation.


Mean-Field Theory of Yielding under Oscillatory Shear
Jack T. Parley, Srikanth Sastry, and Peter Sollich.
Physical Review Letters 128, 198001, 2022.
Mean-field theory for yielding under oscillatory shear, studying the transition between reversible and irreversible dynamics in amorphous materials.


Aging in a mean field elastoplastic model of amorphous solids
Jack T. Parley, Suzanne M. Fielding, and Peter Sollich.
Physics of Fluids 32, 127104, 2020.
Study of aging dynamics in a mean-field elastoplastic model of amorphous solids.


Effects of Heterogeneous Social Interactions on Flocking Dynamics
M. Carmen Miguel, Jack T. Parley, and Romualdo Pastor-Satorras.
Physical Review Letters 120, 068303, 2018.
Study of how heterogeneous interaction networks affect collective motion and flocking dynamics.

title: Research
text:
My current research asks how deep networks can learn hierarchical linguistic structure from examples alone.
I study this question using probabilistic context-free grammars, synthetic languages, inside–outside algorithms, and neural architectures trained on structured data. A central goal is to understand which statistics make parsing learnable, how much data is required, and how these mechanisms relate to language acquisition in large neural networks.
My ICML 2026 paper, Deep networks learn to parse uniform-depth context-free languages from local statistics, studies a controlled family of context-free languages with varying tree topology and ambiguity. The work shows that deep networks can exploit local statistical regularities to construct parse trees, and connects their sample complexity to theoretically predicted correlation strengths.

title: Research
text:
My current research asks how deep networks can learn hierarchical linguistic structure from examples alone.
I study this question using probabilistic context-free grammars, synthetic languages, inside–outside algorithms, and neural architectures trained on structured data. A central goal is to understand which statistics make parsing learnable, how much data is required, and how these mechanisms relate to language acquisition in large neural networks.
My ICML 2026 paper, Deep networks learn to parse uniform-depth context-free languages from local statistics, studies a controlled family of context-free languages with varying tree topology and ambiguity. The work shows that deep networks can exploit local statistical regularities to construct parse trees, and connects their sample complexity to theoretically predicted correlation strengths.

title: Research
text:
Statistical physics of learning
A central theme in my work is the use of statistical-physics ideas to understand learning: phase transitions, sample complexity, scaling laws, and the emergence of latent structure from data.
This perspective is particularly useful for language-like data, where the observable sequence is generated by hidden hierarchical structure. I am interested in how simple models can reveal the mechanisms by which neural networks discover such structure.

title: Research
text:
Previous work in statistical physics
Before moving into AI and language learning, I worked on the statistical physics of amorphous solids, with a focus on aging, yielding, avalanches, and driven disordered systems.
My Ph.D. developed mean-field theories for the aging and yielding of athermal amorphous solids. This work led to first-author publications in Physical Review Letters, Physical Review Materials, and Physics of Fluids. During my subsequent postdoctoral work in Göttingen, I developed mean-field approaches to ductile and brittle yielding transitions beyond standard random-field-Ising-model paradigms.
This background continues to shape my approach to machine learning: I am especially interested in simple models, emergent structure, phase transitions, and the relation between microscopic rules and macroscopic behavior.

title: Research
text:
Methods and tools
My work combines:

probabilistic modeling
statistical mechanics
grammar induction and parsing
inside and inside–outside algorithms
deep learning and transformer models
synthetic data generation
scalable PyTorch implementations
numerical experiments on GPU/HPC systems

title: Jack T. Parley
text:
I am a researcher working at the interface of statistical physics, machine learning, and language acquisition.
My current work studies how hierarchical linguistic structure can be learned from examples alone, combining ideas from probabilistic grammars, inside–outside algorithms, and deep learning. My previous research in statistical physics focused on the emergence of macroscopic structure from microscopic interactions.
